---
title: "Lab 5"
subtitle: "Auditing a Significance Criterion with Monte Carlo: the GreenBowl Platform"
author: "Ihor Miroshnychenko"
institute: Kyiv School of Economics
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    number-sections: true
    highlight-style: github
    code-line-numbers: false
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
---

# Objective

In **Lab 4** you were handed an A/B dataset and had to recover the right
**business decision**. **Lab 5 is one level up: you audit the *tool* that
makes those decisions.**

The GreenBowl data-engineering team shipped a new significance criterion,
`team_criterion`, and wired it into the experimentation platform — it now
decides "ship / don't ship" on **every** A/B test in the company. Before you
let it touch a single roll-out, your job is to **validate it with Monte
Carlo**, exactly as the lecture taught: *generate many experiments with a
known answer, run the criterion on each, and count how often it is right.*

The twist, as in Lab 4: **you do not get to see what the criterion does.**
`team_criterion` is keyed to your student id. Some students received a
perfectly correct test; some received one that is correct **only under
certain conditions**; some received one that is quietly **broken**. The only
honest way to find out which one you hold is to simulate.

You will:

1. Confirm your tools on a known-good reference, then **generalize**
   `simulate_rejections` so it can validate **any** criterion (not just
   `scipy`'s `ttest_ind`).
2. Measure your criterion's **false-positive rate (FPR)** under $H_0$ with a
   Wilson confidence interval — the calibration check.
3. Find the **minimum sample size** from which it is trustworthy (if ever) —
   the "small new city" problem.
4. Measure **power** under $H_1$ and **compare it head-to-head** with the
   trusted Welch reference — is it valid *and* competitive?
5. Run **≈1000 A/A tests on your historical log** and reconcile the result
   with your synthetic findings.
6. Deliver a one-page **go / no-go verdict** on the criterion to the platform
   team.

# General Requirements

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
## The fixed context (same for everyone)

- **Metric:** weekly **ARPU** per user, in UAH — continuous, positive,
  right-skewed (lognormal).
- **Baseline:** $\mu_0 = 250$ UAH.
- **Significance level:** $\alpha = 0.05$.
- **The criterion under audit:** `team_criterion` from `greenbowl_mc.py`,
  keyed to *your* id.
- **The trusted reference:** `reference_welch` from the same module — a
  textbook two-sample Welch $t$-test you may treat as correct.
- **Your "god" generator:** `arpu_sample(mean, n, sigma)` — you decide the
  truth, so you always know the right answer.
:::

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
## Everyone's criterion is different — on purpose

Your `team_criterion` is unique to your id. It might be the textbook Welch
test, a variant that is valid only on large or balanced samples, or one that
is anti-conservative at every sample size. **There is no single verdict shared
across the class** — there is only the verdict your simulations support.
Copying another student's conclusion will be obviously wrong for your
criterion.
:::

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Report Format

Submit the report in **one** of the following formats:

- **Quarto → PDF** (`.qmd` → `.pdf`)
- **Quarto → HTML** (`.qmd` → `.html`, hosted, e.g. on
  [Quarto Pub](https://quartopub.com/),
  [Posit Connect Cloud](https://connect.posit.cloud/))
- **Jupyter Notebook** (`.ipynb`)
- **Google Colab** (share the link with viewing permissions)

The report must contain both code **and** narrative. Every section begins with
a written explanation, not code.
:::

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## What you build vs. what you test

The thing you **build** today is the **Monte Carlo validator**
(`simulate_rejections`, `min_sample_size`, `thousand_aa_tests`) — grown step
by step from Practice 5. The thing you **test** is taken ready-made: your
hidden `team_criterion` and the `reference_welch`. You may use
`statsmodels.stats.proportion.proportion_confint` for the Wilson interval and
`scipy.stats` for the reference. You may **not** replace your validator with a
library power/AA function — the simulation loop and the confidence-interval
verdict must be **your own code**.
:::

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## Honour rule on the data platform

`greenbowl_mc.py` contains the hidden truth in its source. **Do not read past
the `# === instructor section ===` line, and do not call `_reveal`.** The
point of the lab is to reach the verdict *by simulation*, not by reading the
criterion's code. Any report that names the hidden criterion from the source
instead of demonstrating its behaviour scores **zero** for interpretation.
:::

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## Deadline

📅 **June 17, 2026, 23:59**
:::

# Tasks

## Set Up and Sanity-Check Your Tools {#sec-setup}

Download `greenbowl_mc.py` into your working folder and load **your**
criterion and historical log. Use **one** id consistently — your KSE email is
recommended.

```{python}
#| label: load
#| eval: false

from greenbowl_mc import (load_my_criterion, load_my_log,
                          reference_welch, arpu_sample, BASELINE, ALPHA)
import numpy as np
from scipy import stats
from statsmodels.stats.proportion import proportion_confint

STUDENT_ID = "your.email@kse.org.ua"   # <-- put YOUR id here
team_criterion = load_my_criterion(STUDENT_ID)
log = load_my_log(STUDENT_ID)

# both criteria share the same call signature:
#   crit(test, control, alternative="two-sided") -> p_value
demo_test    = arpu_sample(260, 200)
demo_control = arpu_sample(250, 200)
print("team   :", team_criterion(demo_test, demo_control, "greater"))
print("welch  :", reference_welch(demo_test, demo_control, "greater"))
print(f"baseline = {BASELINE}, alpha = {ALPHA}")
```

::: {.border}
1. State your `STUDENT_ID`. Confirm both `team_criterion` and
   `reference_welch` run and return a $p$-value on a sample you generate with
   `arpu_sample`.
2. In one sentence, confirm you understand that **what the criterion does is
   hidden**, and your entire task is to characterise its behaviour by
   simulation.
3. **Do not** form any opinion yet from this single call — one $p$-value tells
   you nothing about calibration. Say why.
:::

## A Universal `simulate_rejections()` {#sec-fn}

In Practice 5 your validator hard-coded `scipy.stats.ttest_ind`. To audit an
**arbitrary** criterion you must pass the criterion **in** as an argument.

::: {.border}
1. Implement

   ```python
   simulate_rejections(criterion, test_gen, control_gen, n_test,
                       n_control=None, n_exps=10_000, alpha=0.05,
                       alternative="two-sided", seed=...)
   ```

   that runs `n_exps` experiments, each time drawing `test = test_gen(n_test)`
   and `control = control_gen(n_control)`, calling
   `criterion(test, control, alternative)`, and counting rejections
   ($p < \alpha$). It must **return** the rejection rate **and** its 95%
   **Wilson** confidence interval (`proportion_confint(..., method="wilson")`).
   - When the two generators have **equal means**, the rate is the **FPR**.
   - When the test generator has a **higher** mean, the rate is the **power**.
2. **Sanity-check the validator on the reference.** Run it with
   `criterion=reference_welch`, both generators `arpu_sample(250, n)`, at a
   comfortable $n$ (say 400). Confirm the FPR's CI contains $\alpha = 0.05$.
   This proves your *loop* is correct before you point it at the mystery
   criterion.
:::

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Reproducibility
Seed your RNG so every run of the report is identical, but **vary the seed**
at least once (Practice 5, "Your turn 1") to see that the point estimate
wobbles — that wobble is exactly why you always report the interval.
:::

## Is Your Criterion Calibrated? (FPR) {#sec-fpr}

Now point the validator at `team_criterion`. Generate $H_0$ yourself (two
groups from the **same** ARPU distribution, equal means) and read off the FPR.

::: {.border}
1. **Balanced, equal-variance, large $n$.** Run the FPR check for
   `team_criterion` with both groups `arpu_sample(250, n, sigma=0.6)` at a
   large $n$ (e.g. 800). Report the FPR and its CI. Is $\alpha = 0.05$ inside?
2. **Small $n$.** Repeat at a small "new city" size — sweep
   $n \in \{5, 10, 20, 50\}$. Does the verdict change as $n$ shrinks?
3. **Unbalanced *and* unequal-variance.** Run the check with a **noisy,
   smaller** test arm and a **tight, larger** control arm — e.g.
   `test = arpu_sample(250, n, sigma=0.8)` with `n_test=200` and
   `control = arpu_sample(250, n, sigma=0.4)` with `n_control=800`. (Means are
   still equal, so $H_0$ holds.) Report the FPR and CI.
4. **Side-by-side.** For each of the three settings above, run
   `reference_welch` on the **same** generators and tabulate
   `setting | team FPR (CI) | welch FPR (CI)`. Where, if anywhere, does your
   criterion's FPR depart from both $\alpha$ **and** the reference?
:::

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## Read the three settings as a diagnosis, not three numbers
A criterion can be calibrated in one regime and broken in another. A bug that
shows up **only** when the groups differ in size and spread points to a
different fault than one that shows up **only** at small $n$ — and a criterion
whose FPR is off in **every** setting is broken outright. Name what your
pattern implies.
:::

## From What Sample Size Can You Trust It? {#sec-minn}

If your criterion is miscalibrated at small $n$ but recovers as $n$ grows,
there is a threshold worth knowing — the "this city is too small to conclude"
line.

::: {.border}
1. Implement `min_sample_size(criterion, test_gen, control_gen, grid, ...)`
   that **reuses** `simulate_rejections`, sweeps the sample sizes in `grid`,
   and returns the smallest $n$ at which $\alpha$ **first** lands inside the
   FPR's confidence interval (print each step, as in Practice 5).
2. Run it for `team_criterion` on equal-mean generators across a sensible grid
   (e.g. `range(5, 201, 5)` for a small-$n$ problem, or wider). Report the
   threshold — or state clearly that **no $n$ in your grid fixes it** (some
   criteria never become calibrated; that is itself a finding).
3. Plot the FPR (with its Wilson band) against $n$, with a dashed line at
   $\alpha = 0.05$. One figure should make your threshold (or its absence)
   obvious.
:::

## Power — and the Head-to-Head vs. Welch {#sec-power}

A criterion that is **valid** is not automatically **worth using**: among
valid criteria you want the most **powerful** one. Generate $H_1$ (give the
test group a real ARPU lift) and measure the true-positive rate.

::: {.border}
1. Using `simulate_rejections` with a **higher** test mean (e.g.
   `arpu_sample(250 + lift, n)` vs `arpu_sample(250, n)`) and
   `alternative="greater"`, measure the **power** of `team_criterion` for a
   couple of (lift, $n$) settings — at least one realistic small lift
   (e.g. +10 UAH) and one clear one (e.g. +25 UAH).
2. **Head-to-head.** On the **same** generators and settings, measure the
   power of `reference_welch`. Put both in a table with their CIs.
3. Interpret **with the FPR in mind**:
   - If your criterion is **valid** (from @sec-fpr–@sec-minn): is its power
     **at least as high** as Welch's, or does it pay for something with lost
     power?
   - If your criterion is **anti-conservative** (FPR $> \alpha$): explain why
     its "higher" power is **not** a real advantage. *(Hint: a criterion that
     rejects too often under $H_0$ will also reject often under $H_1$ — the
     power number is contaminated by the inflated FPR. Power may only be
     compared among **valid** criteria.)*
:::

## ≈1000 A/A Tests on Your Historical Log {#sec-aa}

Synthetic data are only as good as your guess about the distribution. The
lecture's gold standard is to validate on **real logs**: slice them, and on
each slice run an A/A (split users in half with **no** treatment, so $H_0$ is
guaranteed true).

```{python}
#| label: aa-skeleton
#| eval: false

def thousand_aa_tests(log, criterion, alpha=0.05, lift=0.0,
                      alternative="two-sided", seed=1):
    rng, rej, k = np.random.default_rng(seed), 0, 0
    for _, gdf in log.groupby(["city", "cuisine"]):
        arpu = gdf["arpu"].to_numpy()
        idx  = rng.permutation(len(arpu))      # INDEPENDENT split per slice
        half = len(arpu) // 2
        control = arpu[idx[:half]]
        test    = arpu[idx[half:2 * half]] * (1 + lift)   # lift=0 -> A/A
        rej += criterion(test, control, alternative) < alpha
        k   += 1
    lo, hi = proportion_confint(rej, k, alpha=0.05, method="wilson")
    return {"rate": rej / k, "ci": (lo, hi), "k": k}
```

::: {.border}
1. Run `thousand_aa_tests(log, team_criterion)` on **your** log. Report the
   number of slices, the A/A FPR, and its Wilson CI. Is $\alpha$ inside?
2. **Reconcile with @sec-fpr.** The historical split here is **balanced
   (50/50)**. If your synthetic check flagged a problem **only** in the
   unbalanced/unequal-variance or **only** in the small-$n$ setting, does the
   balanced historical A/A reproduce it? Explain in one or two sentences why
   the synthetic stress test can catch a fault the historical A/A **misses**
   (and vice versa). *This reconciliation is the heart of the section.*
3. **Power on a simulated effect.** There is no real effect in an A/A log, so
   simulate one with `lift > 0` (e.g. +3%, +5%, +10%). Report power for
   `team_criterion`, and — if it is valid — compare with `reference_welch`.
4. **The independence trap.** Replace the per-slice `rng.permutation` with the
   **same** fixed split reused on every slice (or sort by `arpu` before
   splitting) and re-run the A/A. Show what happens to the FPR and explain why
   a non-independent split breaks the validation regardless of how good the
   criterion is.
:::

## Final Verdict {#sec-final}

::: {.border}
Write a **single-page memo** (≤ 400 words) to the GreenBowl **platform team**
who shipped the criterion. It must contain:

1. A one-line **verdict**: **APPROVE** / **APPROVE WITH CAVEAT** (state the
   condition — e.g. "only for balanced designs", "only for $n \ge \dots$") /
   **REJECT**.
2. The **evidence** that forces it: the FPR (with CI) in each regime you
   tested, the minimum-$n$ threshold if any, and the power comparison vs.
   Welch — quoted as numbers, not adjectives.
3. A concrete **recommendation**: should the platform adopt it, restrict it to
   certain experiments, or keep using Welch? If you would reject it, say what
   a correct criterion would have done differently.
4. One honest **caveat or limitation** of your own audit (e.g. simulation
   noise at the $n$ you used, reliance on a lognormal assumption, only 320
   slices in the historical log).

It must be readable by an engineer who has **not** taken this course. You are
graded on whether the platform team could act on it without follow-up
questions.
:::

# Grading {.unnumbered}

| Criterion | Points |
|---|:---:|
| Correctness of methodology | 3 |
| Interpretation of results | 3 |
| Code quality and organization | 2 |
| **Total** | **8** |

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Grading Details

**Correctness of methodology (3 pts):**

- `simulate_rejections` takes the criterion as an argument, runs $H_0$/$H_1$
  correctly, and returns the rate **with** a Wilson CI; it is sanity-checked
  on `reference_welch` (1 pt).
- The FPR is measured in **all three** regimes (balanced large-$n$, small-$n$,
  unbalanced unequal-variance) with correct CIs, and `min_sample_size` reuses
  the validator correctly (1 pt).
- Power is measured under $H_1$ and the ≈1000 A/A on the historical log uses
  an **independent** per-slice split with a correct CI (1 pt).

**Interpretation of results (3 pts):**

- @sec-fpr reads the three regimes as a **diagnosis** (where, if anywhere, the
  FPR departs from $\alpha$ *and* from Welch), not as three loose numbers.
- The power section compares TPR **only after** establishing validity, and
  correctly explains why an anti-conservative criterion's power is not a real
  advantage.
- @sec-aa **reconciles** the historical A/A with the synthetic findings
  (why a 50/50 balanced A/A can miss a fault the synthetic stress test
  catches), and the memo's verdict matches the evidence.

**Code quality and organization (2 pts):**

- `simulate_rejections`, `min_sample_size`, and `thousand_aa_tests` are single
  reusable functions used across all sections (the criterion is always passed
  in, never hard-coded).
- Plots have axis labels, titles, a dashed $\alpha$ line, and legible CIs.
- Narrative precedes every code block; each section reads as an argument, not
  a dump of outputs.
:::

# Defense {.unnumbered}

During the defense (5–7 minutes) you will be asked *"What if?"* questions that
probe whether you understand **why** each piece exists, for example:

- *"Your criterion's FPR came out at $0.058$ with CI $(0.053, 0.064)$. Is it
  miscalibrated, or is that simulation noise? What single change settles it?"*
- *"You measured higher power than Welch. Convince me that is a feature and
  not a symptom. What must you check first?"*
- *"Your synthetic check failed but the 1000 A/A passed (or vice versa).
  Which do you trust, and why can the two disagree?"*
- *"Show me — live — what happens to your A/A FPR if I split each slice the
  **same** way every time instead of re-shuffling. Why?"*
- *"From what $n$ would you let the platform run this criterion in a small new
  city? Point to the number on your plot."*
- *"Halve `n_exps` right now. What happens to your confidence intervals, and
  could that flip one of your verdicts?"*

You may also be asked to **change one parameter live** (flip `alternative`,
shrink $n$, change the lift, swap in `reference_welch`) and explain the
resulting shift.
